teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** Regulation 18 of the Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017 (MLR 2017) requires every relevant person to conduct a firm-wide, written risk assessment of the money laundering and terrorist financing risks to which their business is subject. This is a foundational duty — the risk assessment must inform the firm's policies, controls, and procedures.

**Key Requirements:** The risk assessment must (a) identify and assess the risks of money laundering and terrorist financing to which the business is subject, taking into account customers, geographic areas, products and services, transactions, and delivery channels; (b) be kept up to date; (c) be provided to the relevant supervisory authority on request. The assessment must be proportionate — a small high-street solicitor faces different risks from a multinational bank.

**Why This Matters:** The risk assessment is not a paperwork exercise. It determines the level of customer due diligence (CDD) required, the intensity of ongoing monitoring, and the design of internal controls. A firm that fails to conduct or maintain a proper risk assessment commits an offence under MLR 2017, Reg 86 — punishable by fines and, in serious cases, criminal liability.

**In short:** Every regulated business must first assess its ML/TF risks under Reg 18. This risk assessment is the foundation of the entire AML compliance framework — CDD, monitoring, and controls all flow from it.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, regs 18, 86

question: Under Regulation ____ of the MLR 2017, a relevant person must conduct a written risk assessment of the money laundering and terrorist financing risks to which their business is subject.
feedback: Correct. Reg 18 MLR 2017 mandates a written, firm-wide risk assessment. This must be kept up to date and provided to the supervisory authority on request.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, reg 18

question: Match each factor to how it must be considered in the Reg 18 risk assessment:
answer: Customers=The types of customers served and their associated risk profiles; Geographic areas=The countries or jurisdictions in which the firm operates or with which its customers are connected; Products and services=The nature of the products and services offered and their vulnerability to misuse; Delivery channels=The means by which products and services are delivered (e.g., face-to-face, online, intermediaries); Transactions=The types, volumes, and values of transactions processed

options.text: The types of customers served and their associated risk profiles
The countries or jurisdictions in which the firm operates or with which its customers are connected
The nature of the products and services offered and their vulnerability to misuse
The means by which products and services are delivered (e.g., face-to-face, online, intermediaries)
The types, volumes, and values of transactions processed

feedback: Correct. Reg 18(2) requires the risk assessment to take account of all these factors. The assessment must be proportionate to the size and nature of the business.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, reg 18(2)

question: A small firm of solicitors with 4 partners has no written risk assessment for money laundering. When asked by their supervisory authority (the SRA), the partners say: 'We're too small — we know our clients personally and have never had a problem.' How does their position fare under MLR 2017?

options.text: The firm is compliant — small firms are exempt from the risk assessment requirement under Reg 18
options.feedback: Incorrect. There is no small-firm exemption. Reg 18 applies to all relevant persons regardless of size — though the assessment must be proportionate.

options.text: The partners' oral risk assessment based on personal knowledge of clients satisfies the Reg 18 requirement
options.feedback: Incorrect. Reg 18 expressly requires a _written_ risk assessment. An informal, oral assessment does not comply.

options.text: The firm is non-compliant — Reg 18 requires a written risk assessment from all relevant persons, proportionate to the size and nature of the business. Being small does not excuse the obligation
options.feedback: Correct. The requirement is universal. A small firm's written assessment may be simpler and shorter, but it must exist in writing, be kept up to date, and be available to the supervisory authority on request. Failure to comply can result in regulatory sanctions under Reg 86.

options.text: The firm is compliant because the SRA has not formally demanded the risk assessment in a notice under Reg 18(4)
options.feedback: Incorrect. The obligation to conduct and maintain the risk assessment is ongoing and independent of any request. The reference in Reg 18(4) to providing the assessment 'on request' does not mean the assessment need only be created when requested.

feedback: The Reg 18 risk assessment is universal — every relevant person, from sole practitioners to multinational banks, must conduct one. Proportionality means the _scope_ can be modest, but the _written form_ is mandatory. Non-compliance is a breach of MLR 2017.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, regs 18, 86

question:
Spot the legal error in this compliance advice:

'Under MLR 2017, only banks and large financial institutions are required to conduct a written money laundering risk assessment. Solicitors, accountants, and estate agents can rely on their professional judgment and experience without documenting a formal assessment.'
answer: Regulation 18 applies to _all_ relevant persons, not only banks and large financial institutions. The definition of 'relevant person' in Reg 8 includes solicitors, accountants, estate agents, and many other professionals. All must conduct a written risk assessment.
feedback: Found it. Regulation 8 defines 'relevant person' broadly — it includes credit institutions, financial institutions, auditors, insolvency practitioners, external accountants and tax advisers, independent legal professionals, trust or company service providers, estate agents, high-value dealers, and casinos. All must comply with Reg 18.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, regs 8, 18

question: Which TWO of the following are correct statements about the Reg 18 MLR 2017 risk assessment?

options.text: The risk assessment need only be created when the supervisory authority requests it
options.feedback: Incorrect. The risk assessment must be maintained proactively. Reg 18(4) requires it to be provided on request, but the obligation to _create and maintain_ it is ongoing.

options.text: The risk assessment must be kept up to date
options.feedback: Correct. Reg 18(3) expressly states the risk assessment must be kept up to date, reflecting changes in the business and its risk environment.

options.text: The risk assessment must take account of customers, geographic areas, products and services, transactions, and delivery channels
options.feedback: Correct. Reg 18(2) lists these specific factors that must be considered.

options.text: A firm with fewer than 10 employees is exempt from the Reg 18 risk assessment requirement
options.feedback: Incorrect. There is no size-based exemption. All relevant persons must comply — proportionality governs the _scope_, not the _obligation_.

options.text: The risk assessment must be published on the firm's website
options.feedback: Incorrect. The assessment is an internal compliance document. It must be provided to the supervisory authority on request (Reg 18(4)) but need not be publicly available.

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (c). The risk assessment is a living document that must be kept current and cover the specified risk factors. It is the cornerstone of the firm's AML framework.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, reg 18

question: Under Reg 18(4) MLR 2017, a relevant person must provide a copy of its written risk assessment to its __________ authority on request.
feedback: Correct. Each relevant person has a designated supervisory authority under Schedule 1 of the MLR 2017 — for solicitors, the SRA; for accountants, the ICAEW or ACCA, etc. The authority can demand the risk assessment at any time.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, reg 18(4), sch 1

question: Goldman & Co, a medium-sized accountancy firm, carries out its risk assessment and identifies that 30% of its clients are PEPs (politically exposed persons) from high-risk jurisdictions. Under MLR 2017, what must the firm do next?

options.text: Nothing — the risk assessment itself satisfies the compliance obligation
options.feedback: Incorrect. The risk assessment is the starting point, not the end. It must _inform_ the firm's policies, controls, and procedures.

options.text: Resign from acting for all PEP clients to eliminate the risk
options.feedback: Incorrect. MLR 2017 does not require firms to refuse all PEP clients. It requires enhanced due diligence and senior management approval for PEP relationships (Reg 35).

options.text: Report the PEP clients to the NCA immediately
options.feedback: Incorrect. Being a PEP is not itself grounds for a suspicious activity report (SAR). The firm must apply enhanced CDD — a SAR is only required where there is knowledge or suspicion of money laundering (POCA 2002, ss.330–332).

options.text: The firm must use the risk assessment to develop and implement appropriate policies, controls, and procedures — including enhanced due diligence for PEP clients under Reg 35 — proportionate to the identified risks
options.feedback: Correct. Under Reg 19, the risk assessment must inform the firm's policies, controls, and procedures. For PEP-heavy client bases, this means robust enhanced CDD processes, senior management approval, ongoing monitoring, and source-of-wealth enquiries. The risk assessment drives the compliance response.

feedback: The Reg 18 risk assessment is not a stand-alone document — it must be operationalised through policies, controls, and procedures (Reg 19). Identifying high-risk clients triggers enhanced due diligence obligations, not automatic termination or reporting.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, regs 18, 19, 35

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** Customer due diligence (CDD) is the process by which a relevant person identifies and verifies its customers and understands the nature of the business relationship. MLR 2017 establishes three tiers of CDD — simplified (SDD), standard (CDD), and enhanced (EDD) — corresponding to low, normal, and high risk. The firm's Reg 18 risk assessment determines which tier applies.

**Standard CDD (Regs 27–28):** The baseline requirement. A firm must (a) identify the customer and verify their identity on the basis of reliable and independent documents, data, or information; (b) identify any beneficial owner and take reasonable measures to verify their identity; (c) assess and obtain information on the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship. CDD must be conducted _before_ establishing a business relationship or carrying out an occasional transaction (Reg 30).

**Simplified Due Diligence (Reg 37):** Permitted only where the firm's risk assessment has established that the business relationship or transaction presents a low degree of risk. SDD reduces — but does not eliminate — the CDD measures. The firm must be able to demonstrate to its supervisory authority that the lower risk assessment was reasonable.

**Enhanced Due Diligence (Reg 33):** Required where there is a high risk of money laundering or terrorist financing. EDD means additional measures to examine and verify the customer's identity, source of funds, and the background and purpose of the transaction. EDD is mandatory for PEPs (Reg 35), correspondent relationships with non-EEA institutions (Reg 34), and any case identified as high-risk.

**In short:** CDD is risk-based. Low risk = SDD; normal risk = standard CDD; high risk = EDD. The firm's Reg 18 risk assessment governs the classification.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, regs 27, 28, 30, 33, 34, 35, 37

question: Under Regs ____ and ____ of the MLR 2017, a relevant person must identify its customer, verify that identity using reliable and independent documents or data, and assess the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship.
feedback: Correct. Reg 27 (identification and verification of customers) and Reg 28 (specific CDD measures) together define the standard CDD requirement.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, regs 27, 28

question: Match each CDD tier to its correct description:
answer: Simplified Due Diligence (SDD)=Applies where the firm's risk assessment indicates low risk; reduced verification measures are permitted; Standard Due Diligence (CDD)=The default requirement: identify and verify customer, identify beneficial owner, assess purpose of relationship; Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD)=Applies in high-risk situations; additional verification of identity, source of funds, and closer scrutiny of transactions

options.text: Applies where the firm's risk assessment indicates low risk; reduced verification measures are permitted
The default requirement: identify and verify customer, identify beneficial owner, assess purpose of relationship
Applies in high-risk situations; additional verification of identity, source of funds, and closer scrutiny of transactions

feedback: Correct. The CDD framework is tripartite and risk-based. The firm's Reg 18 risk assessment determines which tier applies. EDD is _not_ just more documents — it means a deeper, more sceptical examination.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, regs 27, 28, 33, 37

question: A UK bank is opening an account for a company incorporated in the British Virgin Islands. The company's beneficial owner is a senior government official from a country with high levels of corruption. The bank's internal risk assessment classifies this as high risk. Under MLR 2017, what CDD is required?

options.text: Standard CDD — the company is incorporated in a British Overseas Territory, so normal verification procedures apply
options.feedback: Incorrect. Incorporation in a BVI does not automatically dictate the CDD level. The key factor is the risk assessment, which has identified this as high risk due to the PEP status and corruption risk.

options.text: Simplified Due Diligence — the BVI is a British Overseas Territory with a stable legal system, so the risk is low
options.feedback: Incorrect. The bank's own risk assessment has classified this as high risk. SDD is only permitted where the firm has determined the risk is low. The PEP status and corruption risk in the source country override any BVI low-risk classification.

options.text: Enhanced Due Diligence — this is a high-risk situation requiring additional measures including verification of source of wealth, senior management approval for the relationship, and enhanced ongoing monitoring under Regs 33 and 35
options.feedback: Correct. A beneficial owner who is a senior government official from a high-corruption country is a PEP. EDD is mandatory under Reg 35 — this includes establishing the source of wealth and source of funds, obtaining senior management approval, and conducting enhanced ongoing monitoring. Reg 33 additionally requires EDD in any high-risk case.

options.text: No CDD is required until the account has been active for 30 days
options.feedback: Incorrect. Under Reg 30(2), CDD must be completed _before_ the establishment of a business relationship or the carrying out of a transaction. There is no 30-day grace period.

feedback: The combination of a PEP status and high-corruption jurisdiction triggers both Reg 33 (general EDD) and Reg 35 (PEP-specific EDD). The bank must take additional measures — including source-of-wealth enquiries, senior management approval, and enhanced ongoing monitoring.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, regs 30, 33, 35

question:
Spot the legal error in this statement:

'When a firm has classified a business relationship as low risk, the MLR 2017 permits it to dispense with customer due diligence entirely. No identification or verification of the customer is needed because the low-risk classification means there is no risk of money laundering.'
answer: Simplified Due Diligence under Reg 37 does not mean _no_ due diligence. It means the firm may apply _simplified_ measures — reduced in intensity, but not eliminated. The firm must still identify and verify the customer to the extent appropriate. No risk is never zero risk.
feedback: Found it. Regulation 37 permits 'simplified' due diligence in low-risk cases, but CDD measures are reduced, not eliminated. The firm must still be able to demonstrate it has taken reasonable measures. Zero-risk classifications are generally inappropriate — the statutory regime assumes risk is a spectrum, not a binary.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, reg 37

question: Under MLR 2017, which TWO of the following are MANDATORY triggers for Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD)?

options.text: The customer is a UK-incorporated public limited company
options.feedback: Incorrect. A UK PLC is not automatically high risk. PLCs listed on a regulated market may even qualify for SDD under Reg 37 and Schedule 5.

options.text: The customer is a politically exposed person (PEP)
options.feedback: Correct. Reg 35(2) requires EDD for PEPs — including a PEP's family members and known close associates. Senior management approval is mandatory.

options.text: The transaction value exceeds £1,000
options.feedback: Incorrect. There is no value threshold that automatically triggers EDD. The trigger is _risk_, not value — though high-value transactions may form part of the risk assessment.

options.text: The customer is a UK resident
options.feedback: Incorrect. UK residency is not itself a risk factor. EDD is triggered by elevated risk, not by nationality or residence.

options.text: The firm has assessed the business relationship or transaction as presenting a high risk of money laundering or terrorist financing
options.feedback: Correct. Reg 33(1) requires EDD in any case the firm has identified as high risk, whether or not it falls within the specific mandatory categories (PEPs, correspondent relationships, etc.).

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (e). EDD is mandatory in two categories: (1) specific relationships specified in MLR 2017 — PEPs (Reg 35) and correspondent banking (Reg 34); and (2) any case the firm's risk assessment identifies as high risk (Reg 33).
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, regs 33, 34, 35

question: Regulation _____ of the MLR 2017 requires relevant persons to apply enhanced customer due diligence measures in any case identified as presenting a high risk of money laundering or terrorist financing.
feedback: Correct. Reg 33 is the general EDD provision for high-risk cases. It operates alongside Reg 34 (correspondent relationships) and Reg 35 (PEPs) which mandate EDD in specific circumstances.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, reg 33

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** MLR 2017 imposes specific obligations on relevant persons to maintain robust policies, controls, and procedures (POC) to prevent money laundering and terrorist financing (Reg 19) and to keep comprehensive records of CDD measures and transactions (Regs 40–41). These are the operational backbone of AML compliance.

**Policies, Controls, and Procedures (Reg 19):** Every relevant person must establish and maintain POC that are proportionate to the size and nature of the business and are informed by the Reg 18 risk assessment. This must include: (a) risk management practices; (b) internal controls; (c) CDD and ongoing monitoring; (d) reliance and record-keeping; (e) the monitoring and management of compliance; (f) internal communication; and (g) screening of employees. There must be a nominated officer (MLRO — Money Laundering Reporting Officer) responsible for compliance.

**Record Keeping (Regs 40–41):** A firm must retain copies of CDD documents and supporting records for: (a) at least five years from the date the business relationship ends; or (b) five years from the date of the occasional transaction. These records must be sufficient to enable the reconstruction of individual transactions and the provision of evidence for prosecution of criminal activity.

**In short:** POC are the operational architecture; record keeping is the audit trail. Together, they ensure the Reg 18 risk assessment is translated into effective AML controls.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, regs 19, 40, 41

question: Regulation ____ of the MLR 2017 requires relevant persons to establish and maintain policies, controls, and procedures (POC) to prevent money laundering and terrorist financing, informed by the firm's risk assessment.
feedback: Correct. Reg 19 MLR 2017 is the operational compliance provision. It links back to the Reg 18 risk assessment — the POC must be proportionate and risk-informed.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, reg 19

question: Under Reg 40 MLR 2017, a relevant person must retain records of CDD documents and supporting evidence for a period of at least ____ years from the date the business relationship ends.
feedback: Correct. Reg 40 requires a minimum five-year retention period for CDD records following the end of the relationship or the completion of an occasional transaction. This matches the five-year retention period for suspicious activity report records under POCA 2002.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, reg 40

question: Match each Reg 19 POC element to its required content:
answer: Risk management practices=Systems for identifying, assessing, and mitigating ML/TF risks; Internal controls=Checks on staff handling, segregation of duties, IT security; CDD and ongoing monitoring=Processes for verifying customers and scrutinising transactions throughout the relationship; Compliance monitoring=Oversight of the firm's adherence to AML obligations; Employee screening=Background checks and training to ensure staff are fit and proper

options.text: Systems for identifying, assessing, and mitigating ML/TF risks
Checks on staff handling, segregation of duties, IT security
Processes for verifying customers and scrutinising transactions throughout the relationship
Oversight of the firm's adherence to AML obligations
Background checks and training to ensure staff are fit and proper

feedback: Correct. Reg 19 requires a comprehensive POC framework. The MLRO (Money Laundering Reporting Officer) oversees these elements and acts as the bridge between the firm and the NCA for SAR reporting.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, reg 19

question: A law firm completed a property transaction for a client in 2018. The client relationship ended that year. In 2025, the NCA requests the firm's CDD records for that client as part of a criminal investigation. The firm says it destroyed the records in 2023 — five years after the transaction. Is the firm compliant with MLR 2017?

options.text: Yes — the firm kept the records for 5 years (2018–2023), satisfying Reg 40
options.feedback: Close, but the five-year clock runs from the _end_ of the business relationship, not the date of the transaction. If the relationship ended in 2018, records must be kept until 2023. But if the relationship ended later, the firm's calculation may be wrong.

options.text: Potentially non-compliant — the five-year retention period under Reg 40 runs from the date the business relationship _ends_. If the relationship ended in 2018, destruction was lawful in 2023, and the firm is compliant. If the relationship continued beyond 2018, the firm may have breached the retention obligation
options.feedback: Correct. Reg 40(3)(a) specifies the five-year period runs from the date on which the business relationship ends. The key question is when the relationship ended — if it was 2018, the firm is compliant. If the relationship continued (e.g., the client remained on the firm's books or received further services), the firm may have destroyed records prematurely.

options.text: No — MLR 2017 requires records to be kept indefinitely
options.feedback: Incorrect. Reg 40 specifies a five-year retention period, not indefinite retention. However, the firm must correctly calculate the start of that period.

options.text: Yes — the firm is always entitled to destroy records after five calendar years
options.feedback: Incorrect. The five-year period runs from the _end of the business relationship_, not from a fixed calendar date. The firm must track relationship end dates accurately.

feedback: The Reg 40 five-year retention period starts from the end of the business relationship, not the transaction date. Firms must have systems to track when client relationships formally end. A client who is dormant but not formally off-boarded may still be 'in a relationship' for MLR purposes.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, reg 40(3)(a)

question:
Spot the legal error:

'A firm's AML policies, controls, and procedures under Reg 19 MLR 2017 are a one-off exercise: once the compliance manual is written and approved by the board, the firm has discharged its POC obligations permanently.'
answer: POC obligations are ongoing, not one-off. Reg 19 requires the firm to _maintain_ policies, controls, and procedures — they must be regularly reviewed, updated to reflect changes in the law and the firm's risk profile, and tested for effectiveness. A static compliance manual is evidence of non-compliance.
feedback: Found it. Regulation 19(1) requires firms to 'establish and maintain' POC. This implies a continuous obligation — policies must evolve with the firm's business, its Reg 18 risk assessment, and changes in the regulatory landscape. A compliance manual drafted years ago and never updated falls short of the statutory standard.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, reg 19

question: Which TWO of the following are required by the MLR 2017 record-keeping provisions?

options.text: CDD records must be retained for at least five years after the business relationship ends
options.feedback: Correct. Reg 40(3)(a) specifies the five-year post-relationship retention period.

options.text: All CDD records must be stored in original physical paper form
options.feedback: Incorrect. Electronic storage is permitted, provided the records are accessible and retrievable. Reg 40(5) allows copies of documents, including electronic copies.

options.text: Records must be sufficient to enable the reconstruction of individual transactions
options.feedback: Correct. Reg 41 requires that records of transactions be sufficient to permit reconstruction and serve as evidence for criminal prosecutions.

options.text: Records must be destroyed immediately upon the end of the business relationship
options.feedback: Incorrect. The obligation is to _retain_ records for five years, not to destroy them immediately.

options.text: Records may only be disclosed to the NCA with a court order
options.feedback: Incorrect. Records must be provided to the supervisory authority on request (Reg 18(4)) and to the NCA in connection with SARs or investigations under POCA 2002 without a court order.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (c). The record-keeping regime is designed to create a durable audit trail. Five-year retention and the ability to reconstruct transactions are essential for law enforcement and regulatory investigations.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, regs 40, 41

question: Regulation _____ of the MLR 2017 requires records of transactions to be sufficient to permit the reconstruction of individual transactions so as to provide, if necessary, evidence for prosecution of criminal activity.
feedback: Correct. Reg 41 complements Reg 40 — Reg 40 governs CDD records, Reg 41 governs transaction records. Both require five-year retention.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, reg 41

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Blocks A & B] A firm of estate agents conducts a Reg 18 risk assessment and concludes its business is 'low risk' because it deals exclusively with residential property in a single UK town. The firm applies simplified due diligence to all customers: it takes the customer's name and address but does not verify identity documents, investigate beneficial ownership, or assess the purpose of transactions. The SRA (its supervisory authority) reviews the firm. Is the firm compliant?

options.text: Yes — if the Reg 18 assessment finds low risk, SDD is always sufficient and no further verification is needed
options.feedback: Incorrect. Even under SDD, the firm must still carry out _some_ verification — SDD reduces, but does not eliminate, CDD measures. Taking a name and address without verifying it falls short.

options.text: Yes — residential property in a UK town is inherently low risk under the MLR 2017
options.feedback: Incorrect. The MLR 2017 does not classify entire sectors or property types as inherently low risk. The risk assessment must be specific to the firm's actual business, and even low-risk SDD requires verification.

options.text: No — estate agents cannot use SDD; they must always apply EDD
options.feedback: Incorrect. Estate agents can use SDD where their risk assessment supports a low-risk classification. The problem here is that SDD must still involve _some_ verification measures.

options.text: No — even under SDD, the firm must conduct some verification of identity, identify any beneficial owner, and assess the purpose of the transaction. Simply recording unverified details is insufficient
options.feedback: Correct. SDD is not _no_ due diligence — Reg 37 permits 'simplified' measures, but the firm must still be able to demonstrate to its supervisory authority that it has taken reasonable and proportionate steps. Recording an unverified name and address is not SDD; it is effectively no CDD at all. The firm's Reg 18 assessment may also be inadequate if it reflexively classifies all business as low risk without genuine analysis.

feedback: This consolidates Blocks A and B. The Reg 18 assessment and CDD framework work together: a properly conducted risk assessment informs the CDD level, but even SDD requires identification and verification — just at a reduced intensity. A blanket 'low risk' classification with no verification is a dual failure: inadequate risk assessment _and_ non-compliant CDD.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, regs 18, 27, 28, 37

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block B] Enhanced Due Diligence is mandatory for __________ exposed persons and their family members and known close associates under Reg 35 MLR 2017.
feedback: Correct. Regulation 35 requires EDD for PEPs — a PEP is defined in Reg 35(12) by reference to the FATF definition and includes heads of state, government ministers, senior judges, military leaders, and directors of state-owned enterprises, among others.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, reg 35

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block C] An audit reveals that a bank's CDD records for 200 high-risk clients were accidentally deleted in a server migration. The bank has no backups. The relationships with most of these clients are ongoing. Under MLR 2017, what is the bank's position?

options.text: No breach — record-keeping is a best-practice recommendation, not a legal requirement
options.feedback: Incorrect. Record-keeping under Regs 40–41 is a statutory obligation, not advisory guidance.

options.text: The bank is in breach of Reg 40 — the five-year retention requirement applies, and the bank cannot reconstruct the CDD records. It must immediately re-perform CDD on the affected clients and may face regulatory action for the failure
options.feedback: Correct. The bank has lost records that Reg 40 requires it to retain for ongoing relationships. It must urgently re-verify all affected clients and may face enforcement action from its supervisory authority under Reg 86. The deletion is a compliance failure regardless of whether the bank has committed a money laundering offence.

options.text: The bank is compliant because the deletion was accidental, not deliberate
options.feedback: Incorrect. The MLR 2017 does not distinguish between accidental and deliberate non-compliance. The regulatory obligation is to have systems in place to maintain records — the absence of backups suggests inadequate POC under Reg 19 as well.

options.text: The bank must file SARs for all 200 clients with the NCA immediately
options.feedback: Incorrect. The loss of CDD records does not of itself create a suspicion of money laundering. A SAR requires knowledge or suspicion that a person is engaged in money laundering (POCA 2002, ss.330–332). Record-keeping failures require regulatory reporting, not automatically criminal SARs.

feedback: This consolidates Block C. Record-keeping is a statutory duty, not administrative housekeeping. The failure here is twofold: breach of Reg 40 (lost records) and potential breach of Reg 19 (inadequate IT controls/procedures). The practical remedy is to re-perform CDD urgently, but the bank remains liable for the breach itself.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, regs 19, 40, 86

question: [CONSOLIDATION — All Blocks] Which TWO of the following correctly describe the relationship between MLR 2017 risk assessment, CDD, and POC?

options.text: The risk assessment (Reg 18) and POC (Reg 19) are independent obligations — the risk assessment does not need to inform the POC
options.feedback: Incorrect. Reg 19(1) expressly requires POC to be proportionate to the size and nature of the business _and informed by the Reg 18 risk assessment_. They are interdependent.

options.text: The level of CDD (simplified, standard, enhanced) applied to a customer must be determined by reference to the firm's Reg 18 risk assessment
options.feedback: Correct. The risk assessment is the foundation — it tells the firm which customers, jurisdictions, products, and transactions are low, standard, or high risk, which in turn determines the CDD tier.

options.text: Record-keeping requirements (Reg 40) only apply to high-risk clients
options.feedback: Incorrect. Reg 40 applies to all CDD — simplified, standard, and enhanced. Every client's CDD records must be retained for at least five years after the relationship ends.

options.text: The entire AML framework is risk-based: the firm's assessment of its own risks drives the CDD it applies, the POC it implements, and the intensity of its ongoing monitoring
options.feedback: Correct. This is the central philosophy of the MLR 2017 — a risk-based approach (RBA). The firm assesses its risks, then deploys proportionate controls. One-size-fits-all compliance is insufficient.

options.text: A firm can delegate its Reg 18 risk assessment obligation to an external consultant and take no further responsibility for it
options.feedback: Incorrect. While a firm may engage external expertise, the obligation under Reg 18 rests with the relevant person itself. The firm must own and understand its risk assessment — delegation does not transfer responsibility.

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (d). The MLR 2017 framework is coherent and integrated: risk assessment → risk-based CDD → proportionate POC → record keeping. Each element supports the others. A failure in one (inadequate risk assessment, for instance) undermines the entire framework.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692, regs 18, 19, 27, 28, 33, 37, 40

question: [CONSOLIDATION — All Blocks] The Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017 were made under the European Communities Act 1972 and are commonly cited as SI 2017/____.
feedback: Correct. The MLR 2017 are SI 2017/692. They implemented the Fourth Money Laundering Directive (EU) 2015/849 and were subsequently amended by the Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing (Amendment) Regulations 2019 (SI 2019/1511) to implement the Fifth Money Laundering Directive.
oscoaReferences: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, SI 2017/692
Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing (Amendment) Regulations 2019, SI 2019/1511
